title: 4 Hidar — St. John the Apostle & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "4 Hidar — St. John the Apostle & The Assembly

narrative": "Hidar

St. John the Apostle and Evangelist (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Mercurius (Abu Seifein), Great Martyr

St. Paul the Confessor, Archbishop of Constantinople

The fourth day of Hidar brings the monthly commemoration of St. John the Apostle, and in the month of the Apostolic Vesting his Gospel speaks most clearly about the relationship between love and mission. 'As the Father has sent me, I am sending you' — these words from the Resurrection appearance in John 20, accompanied by the breath of the Holy Spirit, are the Johannine version of the apostolic vesting: the sent-ness of the Son becoming the sent-ness of the community, the divine commission passed through the Son to the disciples, sealed by the gift of the Spirit. In Hidar, John's Gospel is the Gospel of the apostolic commission in its most intimate form — not the solemn legal transfer of authority but the breath of the Risen One on the faces of those He loves, followed by the words that change their identity from disciples to sent ones.

St. Mercurius — Abu Seifein, the Father of Two Swords — is one of the most beloved military martyrs in the Coptic and Ethiopian traditions. He was a Roman soldier of Cappadocian origin who was martyred under Decius for refusing to sacrifice to the gods, and whose sanctity was so great that the tradition assigns to him, among other miraculous interventions, the striking down of Julian the Apostate — the emperor who had attempted to reverse the Christianization of the empire — at the intercessory request of St. Basil the Great. The two swords of his title are variously explained in the tradition: the sword of his military service and the sword of his martyrdom's spiritual warfare. In Hidar's apostolic month, Mercurius the two-sworded warrior is the image of the vested apostolic community: equipped with both the active commission and the spiritual authority to confront the empire's periodic attempts to reclaim the territory the Gospel has taken.

St. Paul the Confessor was Archbishop of Constantinople in the turbulent mid-fourth century — one of the bishops who paid the price of orthodox faithfulness during the Arian ascendancy with repeated exile and final martyrdom. He was twice driven from his throne by imperial pressure supporting the Arian cause, and was eventually strangled in exile in Cucusus. Paul the Confessor's apostolic vesting was a vesting into the specific cost of episcopal faithfulness in an era when the emperor's theology and the Gospel's theology were in direct conflict. He is the bishop who demonstrates that the apostolic commission includes, as one of its essential provisions, the willingness to be removed from the post in which one serves while remaining faithful to the office.

John, Mercurius, and Paul the Confessor together on this fourth day of Hidar present the apostolic vesting in its Johannine, martial, and episcopal expressions. John conveys the commission with the intimacy of the Resurrection breath. Mercurius receives it as a soldier and wields it as two swords — the weapon of military service transformed into the weapon of spiritual warfare. Paul the Confessor receives it as an archbishop and carries it through exile and strangulation without surrendering it. Three forms of the same sent-ness, three modes of the same commission: the apostolic vesting of Hidar equips for all three, and the community entering its fourth day is already being shaped into the readiness for whichever form its own commissioning will take.
